section.title: Clean Food and Tithes
section.sectionRationale: This chapter moves from worship centralization and internal loyalty to the visible, everyday markers of Israel's set-apart identity, mourning customs, diet, and generous tithing, showing that holiness was meant to shape the smallest daily habits, not only the great acts of national worship.
section.thesis: As sons of the LORD, a people holy to Him, Israel must mark their identity even in mourning customs and diet, distinguishing clean from unclean creatures, and must give a tenth of their produce both to rejoice before the LORD and, every third year, to provide for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow.
section.frameNote: Six movements: sons of the LORD, no pagan mourning rites; clean and unclean land animals; clean and unclean water creatures and birds; no carcasses, and no boiling a young goat in its mother's milk; the yearly tithe eaten joyfully before the LORD; and the triennial tithe for the vulnerable.

units.label: Sons of the LORD: no pagan mourning rites

verses.text: You are sons of the LORD your God; do not cut yourselves or shave your foreheads on behalf of the dead,
for you are a people {{qadosh|holy}} to the LORD your God. The LORD has chosen you to be a people for His prized possession out of all the peoples on the face of the earth.
You must not eat any detestable thing.
These are the animals that you may eat: The ox, the sheep, the goat,
the deer, the gazelle, the roe deer, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the mountain sheep.
You may eat any animal that has a split hoof divided in two and that chews the cud.

But of those that chew the cud or have a completely divided hoof, you are not to eat the following: the camel, the rabbit, or the rock badger. Although they chew the cud, they do not have a divided hoof. They are {{tahor|unclean}} for you,
as well as the pig; though it has a divided hoof, it does not chew the cud. It is unclean for you. You must not eat its meat or touch its carcass.

units.label: Clean and unclean water creatures and birds

verses.text: Of all the creatures that live in the water, you may eat anything with fins and scales,
but you may not eat anything that does not have fins and scales; it is unclean for you.
You may eat any clean bird,
but these you may not eat: the eagle, the bearded vulture, the black vulture,
the red kite, the falcon, any kind of kite,
any kind of raven,
the ostrich, the screech owl, the gull, any kind of hawk,
the little owl, the great owl, the white owl,
the desert owl, the osprey, the cormorant,
the stork, any kind of heron, the hoopoe, or the bat.
All flying insects are unclean for you; they may not be eaten.
But you may eat any clean bird.

units.label: No carcasses; no boiling a kid in its mother's milk

verses.text: You are not to eat any carcass; you may give it to the foreigner residing within your gates, and he may eat it, or you may sell it to a foreigner. For you are a holy people belonging to the LORD your God. You must not cook a young goat in its mother's milk.

units.label: The yearly tithe eaten before the LORD
units.note: so that you may learn to fear Him

verses.text: You must be sure to set aside a {{ma'aser|tenth}} of all the produce brought forth each year from your fields.

And you are to eat a tenth of your grain, new wine, and oil, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks, in the presence of the LORD your God at the place He will choose as a dwelling for His Name, so that you may learn to fear the LORD your God always.
But if the distance is too great for you to carry that with which the LORD your God has blessed you, because the place where the LORD your God will choose to put His Name is too far away,
then exchange it for money, take the money in your hand, and go to the place the LORD your God will choose.

Then you may spend the money on anything you desire: cattle, sheep, wine, strong drink, or anything you wish. You are to feast there in the presence of the LORD your God and rejoice with your household.
And do not neglect the Levite within your gates, since he has no portion or inheritance among you.

units.label: The triennial tithe for the vulnerable

verses.text: At the end of every three years, bring a tenth of all your produce for that year and lay it up within your gates.

Then the Levite (because he has no portion or inheritance among you), the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow within your gates may come and eat and be satisfied. And the LORD your God will bless you in all the work of your hands.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart. Applied here to a whole people, marking Israel's difference from the surrounding nations even in mourning customs and diet, everyday practices meant to remind them constantly of their unique identity as God's own.

tahor.gloss: Unclean, ritually impure. Describes creatures unfit for Israel to eat, not necessarily because they are inherently evil, but as a divinely established boundary marker teaching Israel to distinguish and discern in every area of life, including the dinner table.

ma'aser.gloss: Tithe, a tenth. A regular, proportionate offering that trained Israel in ongoing worship, joyful dependence on God, and generosity toward the Levite and the vulnerable, rather than a one-time transaction.

basic.context: As sons of the LORD and a people holy to Him, Israel is given specific dietary boundaries and mourning customs that mark them as distinct from surrounding nations, alongside instructions for a yearly tithe eaten joyfully before God and a triennial tithe set aside for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow.
basic.hermeneutics: These specific food laws and mourning customs were covenant markers for Israel's unique identity as a nation, later fulfilled and reinterpreted in the New Testament, which declares all foods clean, Mark 7:19, Acts 10:15. Their abiding principle, that God's people are called to visible, everyday holiness and generosity, carries forward even as the specific dietary boundary lines do not.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is qadosh, holy, set apart, a status marked even in mourning practices and the difference between tahor, clean, and unclean animals at the table. The ma'aser, tithe, was not a one-time tax but a regular rhythm of worship and generosity, both toward God directly and toward the vulnerable in the community.
basic.typology: The clean and unclean distinctions that once marked Israel apart from the nations anticipate the gospel's declaration that God has made all foods, and all peoples, clean through Christ, Acts 10:9-16, 28, dissolving the old dividing wall between Jew and Gentile, Ephesians 2:14.
basic.christ: Peter's vision of clean and unclean animals in Acts 10 directly echoes this chapter's categories, and its meaning, that God has made the Gentiles clean through Christ, breaks down the very distinctions this chapter established, showing how the gospel fulfills and transforms Israel's old boundary markers into a new, worldwide people of God.
basic.god: God delights to be worshiped joyfully, commanding a feast in His presence rather than mere solemn duty, and He is equally concerned for the practical welfare of the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, showing that true worship and practical generosity toward the vulnerable are never separated in His eyes.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 10:9-16, 28 records Peter's vision declaring all foods, and all peoples, clean through Christ. Mark 7:19 records Jesus declaring all foods clean. Ephesians 2:14 says Christ has broken down the dividing wall of hostility between Jew and Gentile. James 1:27 defines pure religion partly as caring for orphans and widows.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows holiness reaching into the smallest details of daily life, diet, mourning, and giving, training Israel to remember constantly who they belonged to and to care practically for the vulnerable among them. The gospel fulfills this pattern by declaring all foods and all peoples clean in Christ, while carrying forward its call to worship joyfully and give generously to those in need.

context.literary: This chapter follows the warnings against enticement to idolatry in chapter 13 with positive, everyday markers of covenant identity, moving from mourning customs to food laws to the rhythms of tithing, before the festival calendar of chapters 15-16.
context.historical: Cutting oneself or shaving the forehead in mourning was a known Canaanite practice associated with pagan grief rituals for the dead; Israel's prohibition of these customs visibly distinguished their hope in the living God from the surrounding nations' despair.
context.canonical: The clean and unclean food laws given here are directly addressed and transformed in the New Testament, where Jesus declares all foods clean, Mark 7:19, and Peter's vision in Acts 10 uses this very category of clean and unclean to announce that God has made the Gentiles clean through the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give Israel visible, everyday markers, in mourning, diet, and giving, that would continually remind them of their identity as a people set apart and belonging to the LORD, while also providing concretely for worship and for the community's most vulnerable members.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific dietary and mourning regulations describe covenant markers unique to Israel's national identity under the old covenant, explicitly reinterpreted in the New Testament, which declares all foods clean. What remains prescriptive and abiding is the underlying pattern: God's people are called to visible, everyday distinctiveness in how they live, and generous, joyful giving that includes care for the vulnerable.

debates.question: Does 'do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk' (v.21) address a specific pagan ritual practice, and does it establish a broader kosher principle separating meat and dairy?

views.label: A prohibition of a specific Canaanite ritual practice
views.case: Some scholars connect this command to an attested Ugaritic fertility ritual involving cooking a kid in its mother's milk, meaning the prohibition targets a specific pagan practice rather than establishing a general dietary principle.

views.label: The foundation of the later, broader Jewish practice of separating meat and dairy
views.case: Jewish tradition has long read this command, repeated three times in the Torah, Exodus 23:19, 34:26, Deuteronomy 14:21, as establishing the broader kosher principle of not mixing meat and dairy, extending its underlying concern for boundaries and respect for life beyond the single specific act named.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the command reflects God's concern for order, respect for the parent-offspring bond, and separation from pagan practice, whichever specific historical background best explains its original context.

study.originalLanguages: Israel is qadosh, holy, marked apart even in mourning and diet, distinguishing tahor, clean, from unclean creatures at the table as a daily, tangible reminder of belonging to God. The ma'aser, tithe, established a regular, joyful rhythm of worship and provision, both directly to God and toward the vulnerable, rather than a single transactional tax.

typology.type: The distinction between clean and unclean animals
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and transformed in Peter's vision, where God declares all foods, and by extension all peoples, clean through Christ, Acts 10:9-16, 28.

typology.type: The tithe providing for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's consistent call to practical generosity and care for the vulnerable as an expression of true religion, James 1:27.

study.christ: Peter's vision in Acts 10 directly uses this chapter's categories of clean and unclean animals, and its meaning, that God has made the Gentiles clean through Christ, dismantles the very dividing wall these food laws once helped maintain, Ephesians 2:14. Jesus Himself had already declared all foods clean, Mark 7:19, showing that the gospel fulfills rather than merely discards Israel's old covenant boundary markers, gathering a new, worldwide people set apart not by diet but by faith in Him.
study.god: God delights in joyful worship, commanding a feast in His presence rather than mere solemn duty, and He is equally attentive to the practical welfare of the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, revealing that true worship and practical care for the vulnerable are never separated in His character or His commands.

crossReferences.note: Peter's vision uses this chapter's categories of clean and unclean to announce that God has made the Gentiles clean through Christ.
Jesus declares all foods clean, directly addressing the dietary categories established here.
Christ breaks down the dividing wall of hostility between Jew and Gentile, fulfilling what these boundary markers anticipated.
Defines pure religion partly as caring for orphans and widows, echoing this chapter's provision for the vulnerable.

study.oneStory: This chapter shows holiness reaching into the smallest details of daily life, diet, mourning customs, and giving, training Israel to remember constantly whose they were and to care practically for the vulnerable among them. The gospel fulfills this pattern by declaring all foods and all peoples clean in Christ, while carrying forward its call to joyful worship and generous care for those in need.

quiz.q: Why does Moses forbid Israel from cutting themselves or shaving their foreheads for the dead (vv.1-2)?

options.text: Because they are sons of the LORD, a people holy to Him, set apart from pagan mourning customs.
options.note: Verses 1-2 give this exact identity-based reason.

options.text: Because cutting oneself was considered too physically painful.
options.note: Physical pain is not the stated reason; the reason given is Israel's identity as holy to the LORD.

options.text: Because it wasted valuable time during harvest season.
options.note: No such practical concern is mentioned in the text.

options.text: No reason is given for this prohibition.
options.note: Verses 1-2 give an explicit theological reason.

quiz.q: What two characteristics must a land animal have to be considered clean to eat (v.6)?

options.text: A split hoof divided in two, and it must chew the cud.
options.note: Verse 6 states both criteria precisely.

options.text: It must be a domesticated farm animal only.
options.note: Verses 4-5 list both domesticated and wild animals as clean, based on the criteria in verse 6, not domestication status.

options.text: It must be larger than a sheep.
options.note: Size is never mentioned as a criterion in this passage.

options.text: No characteristics are given.
options.note: Verse 6 gives two specific, explicit criteria.

quiz.q: What determines whether a water creature is clean to eat (v.9)?

options.text: It must have fins and scales.
options.note: Verse 9 states this exact criterion.

options.text: It must live only in fresh water.
options.note: No such distinction between fresh and salt water is made; the criterion is fins and scales.

options.text: It must be caught using a net.
options.note: The method of catching is never mentioned as relevant to cleanliness.

options.text: No criteria are given.
options.note: Verse 9 gives an explicit, specific criterion.

quiz.q: What is Israel commanded to do with the yearly tithe of grain, wine, and oil (vv.22-23)?

options.text: Eat it in the presence of the LORD at the place He chooses, so that they may learn to fear Him always.
options.note: Verses 22-23 give this exact instruction and stated purpose.

options.text: Bury it as an offering to the ground.
options.note: No such practice is described; the tithe is to be eaten joyfully, not buried.

options.text: Sell it immediately for maximum profit with no further instruction.
options.note: Verses 24-26 allow converting it to money only when travel distance requires it, and even then it must be spent on a joyful feast before the LORD.

options.text: No instruction regarding this tithe is given.
options.note: Verses 22-27 give detailed instructions.

quiz.q: What happens to the tithe set aside every third year (vv.28-29)?

options.text: It is laid up within the towns so the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow may come and eat and be satisfied.
options.note: Verses 28-29 state this exact purpose and provision.

options.text: It is given entirely to the king's treasury.
options.note: No king or treasury is mentioned; the provision is specifically for the Levite and the vulnerable within the towns.

options.text: It is destroyed completely as a burnt offering.
options.note: The text describes it being eaten by the vulnerable, not destroyed by fire.

options.text: No instruction regarding the third-year tithe is given.
options.note: Verses 28-29 give explicit instruction.

questions: Israel's dietary and mourning customs marked them visibly as 'sons of the LORD,' different from the surrounding nations. What visible, everyday practices in your own life mark you as belonging to God, rather than simply blending in unnoticed?

The yearly tithe was meant to be eaten joyfully in God's presence, teaching ongoing reverence, not merely religious duty. Does your own giving currently feel more like joyful worship or begrudging obligation?

Every third year's tithe provided directly for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow, those without their own means of provision. Who are the vulnerable people in your own community that your resources could directly bless this season?

The distinction between clean and unclean foods gave Israel a constant, daily reminder of their set-apart identity, even at the dinner table. What ordinary, daily moment in your own life could become a deliberate reminder of your identity in Christ?

Moses ties tithing to learning 'to fear the LORD your God always' (v.23). How might regular, generous giving actually be shaping your own reverence for God, rather than merely funding causes you support?

translationNotes.note: Cutting oneself and shaving the forehead for the dead were known Canaanite mourning practices; Israel's prohibition visibly distinguished their hope in the living God from surrounding pagan grief rituals.

'Do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk' may address a specific Canaanite ritual practice, and this same command, repeated three times in the Torah, later became the foundation for the broader Jewish practice of separating meat and dairy.
The stated purpose of the tithe, 'that you may learn to fear the LORD your God always,' frames tithing as a tool for ongoing spiritual formation, not merely a financial obligation.
